architecture.summary: This paper studies how large language models generate structured output (JSON, XML) vs. free-form reasoning under varying task difficulty and model capacity. No novel architecture is proposed; instead, the work empirically isolates format-specific reasoning penalties by comparing chain-of-thought (CoT), structured formats (JSON/XML), information-matched detailed prose controls, and a delayed-structure ablation where models reason freely before reformatting. The key finding is that format compliance competes with reasoning capacity during simultaneous generation, but this effect reverses on easy tasks and disappears for models with sufficient spare capacity.

biblio.title: Capacity, Not Format: Rethinking Structured Reasoning Failures

claims.statement: Models with sufficient headroom absorb JSON constraints without degradation
On hard tasks, Haiku drops 36.2pp with JSON largely due to truncation under standard token budgets
GPT-4o-mini drops 28.0pp with JSON even with extended budgets eliminating truncation, revealing pure capacity competition independent of token exhaustion
On AIME competition math, Opus 4.7 drops from 96.2% to 91.0% under JSON
Delayed-structure ablation recovers most of lost accuracy for capacity-limited models (3-run mean: 80–87%)
Format penalty scales with schema complexity (McNemar p < 0.0001)

Error profiles shift with model capability: Haiku shows reasoning omissions (57% of failures) under JSON, while GPT-4o skews toward arithmetic errors (57% of failures), suggesting capacity competition diverts effort from completing derivations

Prompt length alone does not explain observed degradation; detailed prose controls (information-matched to JSON but without format constraints) show modest prompt-length cost (0.3–8.0pp) but format-specific cost is where models diverge (0.3pp for Sonnet vs. 28.2pp for Haiku)
